one_line_takeaway: On the DUET benchmark, LLM knowledge acquired via SFT unlearns more smoothly and with 10-50% higher retention than knowledge from pretraining alone, and popular facts resist erasure (or even get reinforced) in pretrained models -- so unlearning method evaluations that ignore fact popularity and training-stage origin can be misleading.
key_finding: At N=5%, lr=2e-5 on LLaMA-3.1-8B (Table 1): unlearning popular facts with the Pretrained model INCREASES forget-set ROUGE-L (GA +0.15, GD +0.14, NPO +0.16 vs. the no-unlearning baseline of 0.766), whereas the SFT model shows large decreases (GA -0.73, GD -0.72, NPO -0.57 from a 0.935 baseline) -- the paper's headline 'opposite behavior on popular facts' result. On rare facts both models forget conventionally, but SFT retains much better (retain ROUGE-L 0.931-0.987 for SFT vs. 0.717-0.790 for Pretrained across GA/GD/NPO). Overall the paper reports SFT models achieve 10-50% higher retention quality than Pretrained counterparts at matched learning rates. Full-parameter SFT is shown to be far less stable than LoRA SFT: at NPO, lr=2e-5, city-forget-popular, full-parameter SFT retains a forget ROUGE-L of 0.997 (essentially no forgetting) vs. 0.364 for LoRA SFT, and on multi-domain data full-parameter SFT collapses to forget ROUGE-L 0-0.144 across algorithms (near-total output degradation) while LoRA SFT stays stable. General-capability probes (MMLU, HellaSwag) show at most ~3% deviation across all configurations, indicating unlearning effects stay localized to the targeted domain. Token-rank diagnostics (Table 6) show unlearning rare facts in the Pretrained model shifts average rank from single digits (8.8) to >200 (Delta rank +221.6 for GA), a far larger disruption than for popular facts (Delta rank +7.5), even though popular facts show larger raw log-probability shifts -- illustrating that probability and rank movement diverge as diagnostics.
core_contribution: The paper introduces DUET (Dual Unlearning Evaluation across Training Stages), a benchmark of 28.6k Wikidata-derived question-answer pairs (filtered from 57k triplets across 25 semantic topics) annotated with a fact-popularity score (Wikipedia sitelink counts of subject+object entities, validated against LLaMA-3.3-70B salience judgments with 80.65-81.95% agreement). Using DUET, the authors run a controlled empirical study of how machine-unlearning behavior differs (a) between rare vs. popular facts and (b) between a Pretrained checkpoint and an SFT variant of the same base model (LLaMA-3.1-8B, plus Gemma-7B and Qwen-2.5-7B checks), across three gradient-based unlearning algorithms (Gradient Ascent, Gradient Difference, NPO) and one distillation-based method (UNDIAL). The central, paper-original finding is that SFT models forget more smoothly and retain 10-50% higher quality than Pretrained models at the same learning rate, while Pretrained models unlearning popular facts can paradoxically increase forget-set ROUGE-L (behaving as if unlearning acts as extra fine-tuning on familiar knowledge) and are prone to sudden, unstable collapse. The paper also runs token-probability/rank-shift and hidden-state cosine-similarity diagnostics showing SFT concentrates representation changes on the targeted fact type while pretrained models show little internal adaptation regardless of target.
threat_model: None. This is not an adversarial or security paper: there is no attacker, no threat model, no attack objective, and no defense being evaluated against an adversary. The authors frame machine unlearning generically as an AI-safety/data-governance tool (removing 'unsafe, outdated, or private' knowledge) in their Ethics Statement, but the paper itself performs no red-teaming, no adversarial relearning/extraction attack, and no jailbreak or membership-inference evaluation. It is an empirical benchmark-and-analysis paper about unlearning dynamics as a function of fact popularity and training stage.
limitations: Authors' own Limitations section: (i) Scope is restricted to LLaMA-3.1-8B on the Places-City forget set with a compact retain set; broader domains and larger models are left to future work (Gemma-7B/Qwen-2.5-7B and multi-domain checks are only secondary appendix validations, not the main study). (ii) Popularity labels rely on Wikipedia sitelink counts and LLM-based salience judgments, proxies the authors acknowledge 'may drift over time and across languages.' (iii) ROUGE-L is the primary metric for free-form answers; the authors state complementary factuality/safety judgments and human evaluation are 'planned follow-ups,' i.e., not done here. Additionally (not stated by authors but evident from the text): LoRA is used for both the SFT and unlearning steps in the main results, so the comparison conflates 'training stage' (pretrain vs SFT) with 'parameter-efficient vs full update' in ways the Appendix B.1 full-parameter-SFT ablation only partially disentangles; and popularity is defined solely from English Wikipedia/Wikidata structure, so results may not generalize to knowledge that is popular in the model's training distribution but sparsely linked on Wikipedia.

datasets_used: DUET (introduced in this paper): 28.6k Wikidata-derived QA pairs filtered from 57k triplets, 25 semantic topics, annotated with Wikipedia-sitelink popularity scores
Places-city subset of DUET (9.6k samples) used for the main experiments, with retain-500 and retain-1500 fast-evaluation pools
Domain-balanced multi-domain subset of DUET (500 forget / 500 retain examples) used for the Appendix B.8 generalization check
quantitative_results: LLaMA-3.1-8B, N=5%, lr=2e-5: Pretrain forget ROUGE-L on popular facts rises from 0.766 (baseline) to 0.918/0.908/0.922 under GA/GD/NPO (i.e. increases rather than decreases)
LLaMA-3.1-8B, N=5%, lr=2e-5: SFT forget ROUGE-L on popular facts drops from 0.935 (baseline) to 0.206/0.220/0.364 under GA/GD/NPO
LLaMA-3.1-8B, N=5%, lr=2e-5: SFT retain ROUGE-L on rare-forget setting stays at 0.931-0.987 vs. Pretrain 0.717-0.790
Overall claim: SFT models show 10-50% higher retention quality than Pretrained counterparts at the same learning rate
Full-parameter SFT vs LoRA SFT (NPO, lr=2e-5, city-forget-popular): full-parameter SFT forget ROUGE-L after unlearning = 0.997 vs LoRA SFT = 0.364
Full-parameter SFT on multi-domain data collapses to forget ROUGE-L 0-0.144 across GA/GD/NPO (near-total output degradation) at lr=2e-5
MMLU and HellaSwag deviation from baseline stays within 3% across all tested unlearning configurations
Token-rank shift (GA, forget, Pretrain): rare facts shift average rank from 8.8 (base) to 230.4 (Delta rank +221.6); popular facts shift from 34.2 to 41.8 (Delta rank +7.5)
Popularity-vs-salience agreement (Wikipedia sitelinks vs LLaMA-3.3-70B judgments): 80.65% on 5% forget splits, 81.95% on places-city subset
UNDIAL (self-distillation) on popular split: SFT retain ROUGE-L 0.990-1.000 across lr 1e-6/2e-5/5e-5 vs Pretrain retain ROUGE-L 0.885-0.920
baselines_compared: Gradient Ascent (GA), Gradient Difference (GD), and Negative Preference Optimization (NPO) as the three primary unlearning algorithms compared within-paper
UNDIAL (self-distillation-based unlearning) as an additional algorithm to test generalization beyond gradient-based methods
LoRA-based SFT+unlearning vs. full-parameter SFT+LoRA-unlearning (ablation, Appendix B.1)
Pretrained checkpoint vs. SFT-fine-tuned variant of the same LLaMA-3.1-8B base, treated as the paper's core independent variable rather than an external baseline
Cross-architecture check: LLaMA-3.1-8B (main), Gemma-7B and Qwen-2.5-7B (Appendix) to test model-specificity of findings
